files_to_inspect.reason: Contains SparseCoder class and the sparse_encode function that interfaces with Lasso. Need to understand current parameter handling.
SparsePCA and MiniBatchSparsePCA also use sparse_encode and may need similar changes for consistency.
Contains existing tests for SparseCoder and sparse_encode that will need new test cases.
The example that triggers the convergence warning; need to understand usage pattern.

files_to_modify.description: Add max_iter parameter to sparse_encode function, _sparse_encode helper, SparseCoder.__init__, SparseCoder.transform, DictionaryLearning, and MiniBatchDictionaryLearning. Pass max_iter through to Lasso when algorithm='lasso_cd'.
Add max_iter parameter passthrough for the sparse coding step in SparsePCA and MiniBatchSparsePCA if they call sparse_encode with lasso_cd (for consistency, though they typically use lasso_lars).
Add tests verifying that max_iter is properly passed to Lasso in sparse_encode and SparseCoder when using lasso_cd algorithm.
Update the example to pass max_iter to SparseCoder to resolve the convergence warning.

implementation_steps.description: Inspect sklearn/decomposition/dict_learning.py to understand the full call chain: SparseCoder.__init__ -> SparseCoder.transform -> sparse_encode -> _sparse_encode -> Lasso. Identify all places where parameters are threaded through.
Add max_iter parameter (default=1000) to the _sparse_encode internal function signature. In the lasso_cd branch where Lasso is instantiated, pass max_iter to the Lasso constructor.
Add max_iter parameter (default=1000) to the sparse_encode public function signature and docstring. Pass it through to _sparse_encode calls (both direct and via joblib Parallel).
Add max_iter parameter to SparseCoder.__init__ (default=1000), store as self.max_iter. Update SparseCoder.transform to pass self.max_iter to sparse_encode. Update docstrings and get_params.

Add max_iter parameter to DictionaryLearning.__init__ and MiniBatchDictionaryLearning.__init__ (default=1000) for consistency since they also call sparse_encode internally. Pass through in their fit/transform methods.
Check SparsePCA and MiniBatchSparsePCA in sparse_pca.py for calls to sparse_encode and add max_iter passthrough if applicable.

Add test cases: (1) test that sparse_encode with algorithm='lasso_cd' accepts and uses max_iter, (2) test that SparseCoder with algorithm='lasso_cd' and a custom max_iter doesn't warn about convergence for sufficient max_iter, (3) test that the default behavior is preserved.
Update the plot_sparse_coding.py example to pass a sufficient max_iter value to SparseCoder to eliminate the convergence warning.

assumptions: The Lasso convergence warning in the example is due to insufficient max_iter (default 1000) and can be resolved by allowing users to increase it.
The _sparse_encode function is the internal helper that actually instantiates Lasso, and sparse_encode is the public API that delegates to it.
DictionaryLearning and MiniBatchDictionaryLearning also call sparse_encode internally and would benefit from the same max_iter parameter.
The default value of max_iter=1000 maintains backward compatibility.
risks: Adding max_iter to multiple classes may break existing serialized models (pickle compatibility), though this is standard practice in scikit-learn.
Need to ensure max_iter is only used when algorithm='lasso_cd' and ignored otherwise, or document that it only applies to lasso_cd.
SparsePCA classes have their own max_iter parameter that refers to the outer optimization loop, not the Lasso max_iter — naming conflict needs careful handling.
validation: Run python examples/decomposition/plot_sparse_coding.py and verify no convergence warning appears.
Run pytest sklearn/decomposition/tests/test_dict_learning.py to verify all existing and new tests pass.
Run pytest sklearn/decomposition/tests/test_sparse_pca.py to verify no regressions.
Verify that SparseCoder(dictionary=D, transform_algorithm='lasso_cd', max_iter=5000).transform(X) works without warnings on the example data.
Verify backward compatibility: SparseCoder without max_iter specified still defaults to 1000.

files_to_inspect.reason: Main autodoc module - contains Documenter classes including PropertyDocumenter and ClassLevelDocumenter that handle property and classmethod documentation. Need to understand how members are classified and filtered.
Utility functions for inspecting Python objects - likely contains functions that determine object types (is_property, is_classmethod, etc.) used by autodoc to classify members.
Handles importing and getting members/attributes from modules and classes - the filtering of class properties may happen here.
Existing test target for property documentation - need to understand current test coverage.
Existing tests for property autodoc - need to understand test patterns and add classmethod+property tests.
Main autodoc test file - may contain relevant tests for classmethod/property combinations.
Autodoc directive processing - may be involved in how documenters are selected for members.

files_to_modify.description: Modify the member classification logic to recognize classmethod+property combinations. The key issue is that `type(cls.attr)` for a classmethod-property returns the type of the returned value rather than `property`. Need to handle this by checking `__dict__` directly for classmethod descriptors wrapping properties. Update `PropertyDocumenter.can_document_member()` or add handling in the class documenter to detect these. Also need to ensure docstrings are extracted properly by looking at the underlying property's __doc__ via the class __dict__.

Add or modify inspection utilities to detect classmethod+property combinations. Add a function like `is_classmethod_property()` that checks if an attribute in a class's __dict__ is a classmethod wrapping a property descriptor.
Add test target classes with @classmethod @property decorated methods for testing.
Add test cases for classmethod+property documentation to verify they get properly documented.

implementation_steps.description: Inspect sphinx/util/inspect.py to understand existing type-checking utilities (isproperty, isclassmethod, etc.) and how they work. Understand how safe_getattr works for member access.

Inspect sphinx/ext/autodoc/__init__.py to understand how PropertyDocumenter.can_document_member() determines if something is a property, and how ClassDocumenter.get_object_members() / filter_members() enumerates and classifies class members. Understand the member iteration flow - specifically where classmethod-wrapped properties get lost.

Inspect sphinx/ext/autodoc/importer.py to understand get_class_members() and get_object_members() - these functions enumerate members from classes and may skip classmethod+property combinations because accessing them via getattr returns the property's value rather than the descriptor.

Add a utility function in sphinx/util/inspect.py to detect classmethod-property combinations. This should check `obj.__dict__[name]` to see if it's a classmethod whose __func__ is a property. For Python 3.9+, `classmethod` wrapping `property` is valid.

Modify PropertyDocumenter.can_document_member() in sphinx/ext/autodoc/__init__.py to also match classmethod+property combinations by inspecting the class __dict__ directly rather than relying on the type of the accessed attribute value.

Modify the docstring extraction logic in PropertyDocumenter (likely get_doc() or similar method) to handle classmethod+property by extracting the docstring from the underlying property descriptor found in __dict__ rather than from the attribute value (which would be the return value of the property).

Update the member enumeration in sphinx/ext/autodoc/importer.py (get_class_members or similar) to properly recognize and include classmethod+property members, ensuring they aren't filtered out or misclassified.
Add test target classes with @classmethod @property in tests/roots/test-ext-autodoc/target/properties.py with proper docstrings.
Add test cases in tests/test_ext_autodoc_autoproperty.py to verify that classmethod+property members are properly documented, including their docstrings.
Run existing autodoc tests to ensure no regressions, then run new tests to verify the fix.

assumptions: Python 3.9+ is the target since @classmethod @property stacking only works from 3.9+
In Python 3.9, accessing a classmethod-wrapped property via getattr on the class returns the property's computed value, not the descriptor itself
The class __dict__ stores a classmethod object whose __func__ is a property object, which can be detected
In Python 3.10+, classmethod objects properly inherit __doc__ from the wrapped function, but we need to handle 3.9 where they don't
The PropertyDocumenter is the correct documenter to handle these (they should appear as properties, not as regular attributes)
risks: Different behavior between Python 3.9 and 3.10+ regarding classmethod descriptor __doc__ attribute may require version-specific handling
Accessing class __dict__ directly may not work well with metaclasses or complex inheritance hierarchies
The classmethod+property pattern is deprecated in Python 3.11+, so this fix has a limited useful lifespan
Modifying member classification could have unintended side effects on other descriptor types
The fix may need to handle both regular classes and metaclasses differently since metaclass properties behave differently
validation: Run tests/test_ext_autodoc_autoproperty.py to verify new classmethod+property test cases pass
Run the full autodoc test suite (tests/test_ext_autodoc*.py) to check for regressions
Create a minimal Sphinx project with a class containing @classmethod @property methods and verify they appear in generated documentation
Test with both Python 3.9 and 3.10 to verify behavior on both versions
Verify that the docstring content is correctly extracted and displayed for classmethod+property members

files_to_inspect.reason: Contains _regexp_csv_transfomer (visible in traceback) which splits on commas and then compiles each part as a regex - this is the core of the bug
May contain CSV parsing utilities used for config options
Handles parsing of config files - may be involved in how values are split before reaching the transformer
Referenced in traceback - manages argument parsing including config file arguments
The bad-name-rgxs option is likely defined here in the name checker
May define base options including bad-name-rgxs

files_to_modify.description: Fix _regexp_csv_transfomer to properly handle regex patterns containing commas. Instead of naive comma splitting, use a smarter parsing approach that respects regex syntax (e.g., tracking bracket/parenthesis nesting depth, or trying to compile progressively longer substrings)
Add test case for bad-name-rgxs with regex patterns containing commas like '(foo{1,3})'

implementation_steps.description: Inspect pylint/config/argument.py to understand the current implementation of _regexp_csv_transfomer - it likely does a simple str.split(',') on the input and then tries to re.compile each piece
Inspect how bad-name-rgxs option is defined (likely in name_checker/checker.py) to understand how the type=_regexp_csv_transfomer is used and whether the option definition needs changes

Implement a smarter CSV splitting function in pylint/config/argument.py that handles regex patterns with commas. The approach should: split on commas, then try to compile each part; if compilation fails, join the failed part with the next comma-separated piece and retry. This greedy approach handles cases like '(foo{1,3})' where the comma is inside braces. Alternatively, use a character-by-character parser that tracks nesting depth of parentheses, brackets, and braces to determine which commas are delimiters vs part of regex syntax.

Add unit tests that verify: (1) a single regex with commas like '(foo{1,3})' is parsed as one pattern, (2) multiple regexes without commas still work 'foo,bar', (3) multiple regexes where some contain commas work '(foo{1,3}),(bar{2,4})', (4) edge cases like empty strings

assumptions: The _regexp_csv_transfomer function in argument.py uses a simple str.split(',') to separate patterns before compiling them
The fix can be done entirely within the transformer function without changing option definitions
A greedy compilation approach (try compiling, if it fails join with next segment) will correctly handle all reasonable regex-with-comma cases
risks: A greedy approach might mask actual user errors where they forgot a comma between two separate patterns and the concatenated string happens to be valid regex
The parsing heuristic might not handle all edge cases (e.g., commas in character classes [,] or escaped commas)
Changes to CSV parsing could affect other options that also use _regexp_csv_transfomer but where the simple split behavior was correct
validation: Run the failing case from the issue: configure bad-name-rgxs = '(foo{1,3})' and verify pylint no longer crashes
Run existing tests to ensure no regressions: pytest tests/config/
Test multiple patterns with and without commas in regex: bad-name-rgxs = '(foo{1,3}),bar,(baz{2,4})'
Test that the existing behavior for simple comma-separated patterns still works: bad-name-rgxs = 'foo,bar,baz'
Run the full test suite to check for unexpected side effects

files_to_modify.description: Remove the module-level ROOT constant that uses __file__. Inline the Path(__file__).parent computation at the point of use (likely in the DjangoTemplates or Jinja2 renderer class where ROOT is referenced), computing it lazily instead of at module import time.

In get_git_changeset, wrap the use of __file__ in a try/except (catching NameError) so that if __file__ is not defined, the function returns None (same as other failure modes like git not being available).

Remove the module-level CURRENT_DIR constant. Create a private function _builtin_template_path(name) that computes Path(__file__).parent / 'templates' lazily. In ExceptionReporter, move html_template_path and text_template_path from class-level attributes to being set in __init__ as defaults (only if not already set on the class/instance), so subclasses can still override them.

Change CommonPasswordValidator.DEFAULT_PASSWORD_LIST_PATH from a class-level constant to being computed lazily - either set it as default in __init__ if not provided, or use a property/lazy approach. The key is that Path(__file__) is not evaluated at class definition time.

implementation_steps.description: Edit django/forms/renderers.py: Remove the module-level `ROOT = Path(__file__).parent` constant. Find where ROOT is used (likely to construct template directory paths for the built-in DjangoTemplates/Jinja2 renderers) and replace with an inline `Path(__file__).parent` computation inside the method/property where it's needed, so it's evaluated lazily at call time rather than import time.

Edit django/utils/version.py: In the get_git_changeset function, add a try/except around the code that accesses __file__. If a NameError is raised (because __file__ is not defined), return None. This treats the missing __file__ scenario the same as a failure to run git.

Edit django/views/debug.py: (a) Remove the module-level `CURRENT_DIR = Path(__file__).resolve().parent` constant. (b) Create a module-level private function `_builtin_template_path(name)` that returns `Path(__file__).resolve().parent / 'templates' / name` (computing the path lazily when called). (c) In ExceptionReporter.__init__, add logic to set self.html_template_path and self.text_template_path to defaults from _builtin_template_path if they haven't been overridden on the class. This preserves the override mechanism from #32105 while avoiding __file__ access at class definition time.

Edit django/contrib/auth/password_validation.py: Move DEFAULT_PASSWORD_LIST_PATH from a class constant on CommonPasswordValidator to a default computed in __init__. In __init__, if password_list_path is not provided (is the sentinel default), compute `Path(__file__).resolve().parent / 'common-passwords.txt.gz'` and assign it. This way __file__ is only accessed when CommonPasswordValidator is instantiated, not when the module is imported or the class is defined.

Since all integers are finite, and all rationals are finite, and even/odd imply integer, adding 'rational -> finite' or even 'algebraic -> finite' to the existing rule set should fix the chain.

files_to_inspect.reason: The traceback shows the crash originates from runserver.py line 116 where 'Performing system checks\u2026' is written - this is the primary file mentioned in the issue
The traceback includes autoreload.py - may contain ellipsis characters in output strings
Contains the write method that outputs to stdout - may need to inspect for unicode handling, and may contain ellipsis characters
Staticfiles has its own runserver command that may also contain ellipsis characters

files_to_modify.description: Replace the Unicode horizontal ellipsis character (\u2026) with three ASCII dots (...) in the 'Performing system checks' output string and any other output strings containing ellipsis characters

implementation_steps.description: Inspect django/core/management/commands/runserver.py to find all instances of the Unicode ellipsis character (\u2026 or '…') and replace them with three ASCII periods ('...')

Search the entire codebase for other instances of the Unicode ellipsis character (\u2026 or '…') in Python source files that could be written to stdout/stderr, and replace them with ASCII equivalents. Likely candidates include autoreload.py, base.py, and other management command files.
Verify no other management commands or output-producing files contain Unicode ellipsis characters that would cause the same crash on Windows with cp437 encoding

assumptions: The Unicode ellipsis character (\u2026) was introduced in Django 2.2 or later since the issue reporter says it worked in Django 2.1.7
The primary (and possibly only) occurrence is in django/core/management/commands/runserver.py in the 'Performing system checks…' string
There may be additional occurrences of \u2026 in other files that also write to stdout/stderr
risks: There could be ellipsis characters in translation/locale files that also need to be addressed, though those are typically handled differently
Simply replacing \u2026 with '...' may not catch all non-ASCII characters that could cause similar issues on Windows
validation: Grep the entire codebase for the Unicode ellipsis character (\u2026 or the literal '…') to ensure all instances in output strings are replaced
Run 'manage.py runserver' on a Windows system with code page 437 (default) to verify no UnicodeEncodeError occurs
Run the existing Django test suite to ensure no tests break from the string changes
